does: This paper introduces LocalValueBench, an extensible benchmark for evaluating whether large language models align with localized values and ethical standards, specifically developed for Australian contexts. The work creates a comprehensive question set grounded in a novel typology for ethical reasoning, employs prompt engineering strategies to probe value alignment across multiple dimensions, and develops quantitative evaluation criteria that measure deviations from local values. The authors conduct comparative analysis of three commercial LLMs from US vendors, revealing significant differences in their adherence to Australian ethical and legal standards, and demonstrate that existing globally-focused benchmarks inadequately capture regional value priorities.
connects: This work extends prior research on LLM evaluation and alignment beyond widely-used benchmarks like MMLU and HellaSwag that primarily reflect Western, particularly American, perspectives. It responds to growing concerns about cultural bias in AI evaluation and builds on existing alignment frameworks that assess helpfulness, harmlessness, and honesty, but localizes these concepts. The paper connects to work on constitutional AI and value-based training, while acknowledging limitations of one-size-fits-all evaluation approaches. It engages with regulatory and governance discussions around AI safety that emphasize jurisdiction-specific concerns, building on prior work showing that LLMs exhibit systematic biases reflecting their training data provenance.
enables: This benchmark framework enables regulators and policymakers worldwide to develop context-specific evaluation standards rather than relying solely on international measures, potentially driving demand for region-aligned model variants. The extensible methodology provides a template for other countries and communities to assess and hold LLM developers accountable to local legal and ethical requirements. The work points toward future multi-cultural benchmarking ecosystems and could motivate development of LLMs explicitly trained on diverse ethical frameworks. It also enables researchers to study whether and how model developers can achieve simultaneous alignment with multiple, sometimes conflicting, value systems across different jurisdictions.
